# Compliant Control {.section-divider}

## Why Position-Only Policies Are Not Enough

::: {.two-column-balanced}
::: {.compact}
- Position commands alone do not specify interaction behavior.
- The same motion can be safe or unsafe depending on stiffness.
- Contact-rich manipulation needs motion **and** mechanical response [@villani2016force].
:::

![](assets/figures/position_only_limitations.svg){width=100%}
:::

::: {.notes}
Main point: position commands alone do not define how the robot behaves when the environment resists.

Intuition: the same target pose can be gentle or dangerous depending on stiffness and force response.

Warning: do not frame this as "position control is bad"; it is insufficient by itself for many contact-rich tasks.
:::

---

## Position, Force, and Compliant Control

::: {.two-column-balanced}
::: {.compact}
- **Position control** — track desired motion. *"Where should I be?"*
- **Force control** — regulate interaction force. *"How hard should I push?"*
- **Compliant control** — shape the *relation* between force and motion. *"How should they trade off?"* [@whitney1987force]
:::

::: {.equation-card}
$$
\text{position: } x \to x_{des}
\qquad
\text{force: } F \to F_{des}
$$

$$
\text{compliance: } F \longleftrightarrow x
$$
:::
:::

::: {.notes}
Main point: distinguish the control objectives before introducing impedance and admittance.

Intuition: position asks "where should I be?", force asks "how hard should I push?", compliance asks "how should motion and force relate?".

Warning: real robot controllers often mix these categories, so treat them as conceptual anchors rather than strict boxes. The historical perspective is Whitney's force-control survey.
:::

---

## Impedance Control Intuition

::: {.two-column-balanced}
::: {.compact}
Impedance defines the **force the robot generates in response to a motion error** [@hogan1985impedance]:

- high stiffness: precise but less forgiving
- low stiffness: safer but less accurate
- damping: dissipates energy, smooths response
:::

::: {.equation-card}
$$
F = K_d\,\Delta x + D_d\,\Delta\dot{x} + M_d\,\Delta\ddot{x}
$$

motion error $\Delta x \;\Rightarrow\;$ commanded force $F$
:::
:::

::: {.notes}
Main point: impedance control shapes the apparent mechanical behavior of the robot — it maps a motion error to a force.

Intuition: use the spring-damper analogy: stiffness is how strongly the robot resists displacement, damping is how it resists velocity, and the (virtual) mass is how it resists acceleration. This is Hogan's original formulation.

Warning: high stiffness is not automatically better for precision if contact uncertainty creates large forces. Pure impedance control assumes a torque-controlled, backdrivable robot.
:::

---

## Admittance Control Intuition

::: {.two-column-balanced}
::: {.compact}
Admittance maps a **measured external force to a motion correction** — the inverse causality.

Useful when the low-level interface accepts **position or velocity** commands (as on most industrial arms).
:::

::: {.equation-card}
$$
\Delta x = Y(s)\,F_{ext},
\qquad
Y(s) = \frac{1}{M_d s^2 + D_d s + K_d}
$$

measured force $F_{ext} \;\Rightarrow\;$ motion $\Delta x$
:::
:::

::: {.notes}
Main point: admittance control converts measured forces into motion commands.

Intuition: if the environment pushes on the robot, the admittance law decides how much the reference should move in response.

Warning: students often invert impedance and admittance; impedance maps motion to force, admittance maps force to motion. The next slide makes the duality explicit.
:::

---

## Impedance vs Admittance: A Duality of Causality

![](assets/figures/impedance_admittance_duality.svg){width=100%}

::: {.compact}
Same target dynamics, opposite causality — the choice is driven by the **robot interface**, not the task [@ott2010unified].
:::

::: {.notes}
Main point: impedance and admittance are two realizations of the *same* desired second-order dynamics; they differ in which variable is the input and which the actuator interface can accept.

Intuition: impedance needs a torque-controlled, backdrivable robot — you command force. Admittance needs a position or velocity interface and a force sensor — you measure force and command motion. Ott, Mukherjee and Nakamura show they can even be blended and switched online.

Why this matters here: our case-study arm (the Doosan) exposes a velocity command interface and has a wrist force sensor, so it is naturally an *admittance*-controlled system. That is the direct reason the next section builds a variable-admittance controller rather than an impedance one.
:::

---

## Mass-Spring-Damper Equation

::: {.two-column-balanced}
::: {.compact}
A common admittance model:

$$
M_d \Delta\ddot{x}_r + D_d \Delta\dot{x}_r + K_d \Delta x_r = F_{ext}
$$

The controller **simulates** how a virtual mass-spring-damper would move under the measured force.

Damping is usually derived from a **damping ratio**: $D_d = 2\zeta\sqrt{M_d K_d}$.
:::

![](assets/figures/msd_step_response.svg){width=100%}
:::

::: {.notes}
Main point: present the admittance equation as a desired dynamic relation, not a literal physical mass-spring-damper attached to the robot.

Intuition: the controller simulates how a virtual mass, damper, and spring would move under the measured external force. The figure shows the consequence: the damping ratio zeta sets the transient — too little and the reference overshoots and oscillates, around one it rises cleanly, too much and it is sluggish. Tying damping to zeta (rather than treating D as free) keeps the system well-damped as stiffness changes, which is exactly the parameterization the case study uses.

Warning: choosing gains poorly can produce unstable or sluggish interaction, especially with delays and noisy force sensing.
:::

---

## Variable Stiffness and Damping

::: {.two-column-balanced}
::: {.compact}
Instead of fixed gains, adapt them to task phase and observations [@buchli2011learning; @abudakka2020variable]:

$$
K_d = K_d(t, o_t),
\qquad
\zeta = \zeta(t, o_t)
$$

- approach **stiff** in free space
- go **compliant** near contact
- this is *task-dependent compliance*
:::

::: {.takeaway}
A learned policy can output $(K_d, \zeta)$ as part of its action — exactly the bridge to the next section.
:::
:::

::: {.notes}
Main point: variable gains allow the robot to be stiff or compliant depending on task phase and observations. There is a whole line of work on learning these gains — Buchli et al. introduced learning variable impedance, and Abu-Dakka and Saveriano survey the modern landscape.

Intuition: during insertion, the robot may approach stiffly in free space and become more compliant near contact.

Warning: changing gains online is powerful but risky; abrupt or unconstrained gain changes can destabilize behavior. This is the motivation for the safety guardrails on the next slide and the bounded learned gains in the case study.
:::

---

## Stability and Safety Intuition

::: {.two-column-balanced}
::: {.compact}
- Learned parameters must stay within **safe bounds**.
- Fast changes in stiffness or damping can destabilize interaction.
- **Passivity** / energy-based reasoning gives guardrails: keep stored energy bounded so the controller cannot inject energy into contact.

$$
K_{min} \preceq K_d \preceq K_{max},
\qquad
\dot{V} \le F_{ext}^\top \dot{x}
$$
:::

![](assets/figures/compliance_safety_bounds.svg){width=100%}
:::

::: {.takeaway}
A network can *propose* gains; a safety filter must *enforce* ranges, rate limits, and energy bounds before execution.
:::

::: {.notes}
Main point: learned control parameters must be constrained by safety and stability reasoning.

Intuition: a neural network can propose gains, but a safety filter should enforce allowed ranges and rate limits. The passivity condition — the controller's stored energy never grows faster than the mechanical power the environment supplies — is the energy-based version of the same guardrail.

Warning: empirical success is not the same as stability; contact tasks need conservative deployment assumptions. The case study turns this slide into concrete clipping, force-saturation, and impact-latch behaviors.
:::
